key_insight: Residual stress in a thin film can be predicted by modeling relaxation through dislocation pile-ups at the film-substrate interface, which yields a stress profile that depends strongly on geometry and the initial stress shape.
engineering_relevance: This gives thin-film designers a mechanics-based way to anticipate where stress relaxes most, helping reduce failure risk in constrained coatings and layered materials.
method: Model plastic strain via dislocation density in a screw-dislocation pile-up.
Derive a singular integro-differential equation from force balance and the residual stress profile.
Solve numerically with a collocation method for constant, linear, parabolic, and exponential initial stresses.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports numerical solutions across multiple initial stress profiles and shows that the resulting residual stress distribution and dislocation counts vary with thickness-to-width ratio and initial stress shape.
watch_item: The model assumes a specific pile-up mechanism at an impenetrable interface, so results may be sensitive to boundary conditions and may need extension for more complex microstructures or material behaviors.
paper_title: Residual stress gradient in a thin film within the dislocation pile-up theory
